coredata.dc:title: Cooperative coupling strategy for constructing 0D/2D carbon nitride composites with strengthened chemical interaction for enhanced photocatalytic applications
coredata.dc:creator: Li, Kunqiao
Jiang, Yanqiu
Rao, Wei
Li, Yudong
Liu, Xing
Zhang, Jian
Xu, Xianzhu
Lin, Kaifeng
coredata.dc:description: It is difficult for hydrophobic g-C3N4 to construct tight heterogeneous connection with hydrophilic carbon dots (CDs), resulting in deficient charge transfer and restricted interfacial attachment between two components. Doping is an efficient strategy to firmly strengthen the hybridization of g-C3N4/CDs composites by inducing robust π–π conjugates or covalent chemical bonds for strong CDs anchoring to g-C3N4. In this work, a novel CDs anchored and S, Cl co-doped carbon nitride (CSCl-C3N4) nanosheet is rationally designed and successfully fabricated for strong interfacial connection of g-C3N4/CDs. During the formation process, chlorine atoms are doped within the CDs and C-Cl covalent bond is detected. The strong interaction anchors CDs on g-C3N4 and endowsheterojunctions more compact connection. Systematic analyses demonstrate that CSCl-C3N4 exhibits remarkably enhanced photocatalytic performance for water splitting. Loading 0.5 wt% CDs to S, Cl co-doped C3N4 achieves the optimal photocatalytic activity among a series of samples (192.49 μmol∙h−1), as evidenced by 25 times that of pristineC3N4 for generating hydrogen. Furthermore, the composite also exhibits considerable photocatalytic activity for photodegradation of tetracycline, in which the degradation rate is 15.3 times that of pristine C3N4. Densityfunctionaltheorycalculations and experimental results are employed to investigate the doping positions of heteroatoms withincomposites. ∙O2 − was verified for predominating during photodegradation process to remove tetracycline by radical scavenge experiments. Based on the significant performance of CSCl-C3N4, the possible mechanism of photocatalytic reactions is put forward. The study provides a novel perspective for modifying nanostructures and constructing heterogeneous interfaces with compact connection via combining anchoring of CDs and doping strategies. The newly fabricated CSCl-C3N4 is a considerable photocatalyst in multifunctional applications for solar energy conversion and pollution abatement.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) XRD patterns of 0.5CSCl-C3N4, SCl-C3N4, Cl-C3N4, S-C3N4 and pristine carbon nitride samples; (b) Attenuated total reflection Fourier transform infrared (ATR-FTIR) spectra of 0.5CSCl-C3N4, SCl-C3N4, Cl-C3N4, S-C3N4 and pristine carbon nitride samples; (c) AFM image of 0.5CSCl-C3N4 sample and (d) corresponding thickness analysis taken from the white line.
(a) TEM and (b) HRTEM images of 0.5CSCl-C3N4 sample; (c-d) EDS elemental mappings of 0.5CSCl-C3N4 sample.

(a) N2 adsorption–desorption isotherms; (b) BJH pore-size distribution; (c) High-resolution C1s XPS of 0.5CSCl-C3N4 and pristine carbon nitride samples; (d) High-resolution N1s XPS of 0.5CSCl-C3N4 and pristine carbon nitride samples; (e) High-resolution S 2p XPS of 0.5CSCl-C3N4 sample; (f) High-resolution Cl 2p XPS of 0.5CSCl-C3N4 sample.

(a) UV–vis absorption spectra of 0.5CSCl-C3N4, SCl-C3N4, Cl-C3N4, S-C3N4 and pristine carbon nitride samples; (b) CDs suspension under indoor light and ultraviolet light; (c) Photoluminescence (PL) emission spectra of 0.5CSCl-C3N4, SCl-C3N4, Cl-C3N4, S-C3N4 and pristine carbon nitride samples; (d) florescence lifetimes of 0.5CSCl-C3N4 and pristine g-C3N4.
Doping sites with different forming energy for the S and Cl atoms from DFT calculations.

(a) Transient photocurrent responses of 0.5CSCl-C3N4, SCl-C3N4, Cl-C3N4, S-C3N4 and pristine carbon nitride samples under intermittent illumination; (b) EIS Nyquist plots of 0.5CSCl-C3N4, SCl-C3N4, Cl-C3N4, S-C3N4 and pristine carbon nitride samples; (c) H2 evolution rates of of 0.5CSCl-C3N4, SCl-C3N4, Cl-C3N4, S-C3N4 and pristine carbon nitride samples (error bars); (d) Photocatalytic activities of 0.5CSCl-C3N4, SCl-C3N4, Cl-C3N4, S-C3N4 and pristine carbon nitride samples based on the photocatalytic degradation of TC under visible light irradiation (error bars).

Linear fittings of (a) pseudo-first-order and (b) pseudo-second-order kinetics model for photocatalytic degradation of TC by different samples; (c-d) photodegradation of TC with 0.5CSCl-C3N4 in cycling runs.

(a) Mott-Schottky (MS) plots with the flat band potential; (b) Photocatalytic activities based on the photocatalytic degradation of TC using different scavengers; EPR spectra of the (c) DMPO-∙OH and (d) DMPO-∙O2– adducts recorded with 0.5CSCl-C3N4 under visible light irradiation.

head.ce:title: Cooperative coupling strategy for constructing 0D/2D carbon nitride composites with strengthened chemical interaction for enhanced photocatalytic applications

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: It is difficult for hydrophobic g-C3N4 to construct tight heterogeneous connection with hydrophilic carbon dots (CDs), resulting in deficient charge transfer and restricted interfacial attachment between two components. Doping is an efficient strategy to firmly strengthen the hybridization of g-C3N4/CDs composites by inducing robust π–π conjugates or covalent chemical bonds for strong CDs anchoring to g-C3N4. In this work, a novel CDs anchored and S, Cl co-doped carbon nitride (CSCl-C3N4) nanosheet is rationally designed and successfully fabricated for strong interfacial connection of g-C3N4/CDs. During the formation process, chlorine atoms are doped within the CDs and C-Cl covalent bond is detected. The strong interaction anchors CDs on g-C3N4 and endowsheterojunctions more compact connection. Systematic analyses demonstrate that CSCl-C3N4 exhibits remarkably enhanced photocatalytic performance for water splitting. Loading 0.5 wt% CDs to S, Cl co-doped C3N4 achieves the optimal photocatalytic activity among a series of samples (192.49 μmol∙h−1), as evidenced by 25 times that of pristineC3N4 for generating hydrogen. Furthermore, the composite also exhibits considerable photocatalytic activity for photodegradation of tetracycline, in which the degradation rate is 15.3 times that of pristine C3N4. Densityfunctionaltheorycalculations and experimental results are employed to investigate the doping positions of heteroatoms withincomposites. ∙O2 − was verified for predominating during photodegradation process to remove tetracycline by radical scavenge experiments. Based on the significant performance of CSCl-C3N4, the possible mechanism of photocatalytic reactions is put forward. The study provides a novel perspective for modifying nanostructures and constructing heterogeneous interfaces with compact connection via combining anchoring of CDs and doping strategies. The newly fabricated CSCl-C3N4 is a considerable photocatalyst in multifunctional applications for solar energy conversion and pollution abatement.

ce:section.ce:para: Hydrogen functions as a crucial sustainable and green energy carrier of great concern on account of the only one by-product (water) and high energy density. In order to meet energy and environment and energy demands, this ideal secondary energy source has been applied frequently and of great concern.[1-6] Nowadays, solar-driven photocatalytic H2 evolution via water splitting on semiconductors is considered as a promising and clean technology to utilize solar energy sustainably.[7-10]

More importantly, tetracycline (TC), one of pharmaceuticals and personal care products (PPCPs), are continually detected in a variety of environmental matrixes.[11,12] The increasing concentration of tetracycline from ambient waterways causes antibiotic resistance problem and potentially adverse effects on human health and natural ecosystems.[13] Nevertheless, tetracycline can hardly be decomposed effectively via traditional wastewater treatment technologies.

Numerous studies of photocatalysts focus on the performance of hydrogen evolution, and some on photo-degradation. Nevertheless, photoinduced electrons and holes, as two kinds of active particles, are generated in the photocatalytic process. Photogenerated electrons are mainly involved in the process of hydrogen evolution, while the photo-degradation takes advantage of the oxidability of holes, thereby the activities of these two photoinduced active species are characterized respectively. Thus it is an important work on how to make full use of these two active species for producing hydrogen or degrading pollutants when required. Since the environmental and energy crisis is a continuous challenge, which relates to the vital interests of all mankind, how to effectively solve this problem via a sustainable development strategy is a serious problem for researchers. As a powerful method of environmental remediation and energy conversion, multifunctional photocatalysis has attracted wide attention in the world. Accordingly, a novel environmentally compatible and energy-efficient technology for multi-functional applications is urgently required. Hitherto, numerous candidate materials, TiO2 for instance, are employed in photocatalytic reactions.[14,15] Owing to the limited utilization of solar energy of aforementioned photocatalysts, sunlight-driven materials are developed preferentially to address aforementioned issue for photocatalytic water reactions.

As a metal-free polymeric photocatalyst, graphitic carbon nitride (g-C3N4) has recently drawn significant attention due to the visible light-response and favorable chemical stability.[16-20] Therefore, this photocatalyst with layered structure is applied in solar energy conversation and environmental remediation and so forth.[21-23] Nonetheless, the three challenging issues of C3N4-based materials that poor visible light utilization, rapid recombination of photoinduced electron-hole pairs and small specific surface area has limited practical applications.[24] Various strategies have been developed for purpose of enhancing the photocatalytic performance of carbon nitride, including morphological control, heteroatom doping, heterointerface engineering, polynary hybridization, build heterostructure construction or compositing with other nanomaterials.[10,25-41] Notably, constructing g-C3N4-based composites for effective multiple-effect photocatalytic applications and investigating the relation between improved photocatalytic properties and structure is of great significance. Otherwise, it is quite difficult for photocatalysts to achieve considerable performance and efficiency without the multi-functional technology in photocatalytic pollutants degradation and hydrogen evolution.

Carbon dots (CDs), a desirable zero-dimensional carbon-based nanomaterial, exhibit extraordinary up-converted photoluminescence (UCPL), efficient photogenerated charge transfer, in conjunction with broad light harvesting.[42,43] Moreover, CDs provide various facilitations for photocatalytic reactions, for instance, serving as the energy conversion units and electron acceptors to accelerate photoinduced charge carrier separation efficiently.[29,44-50]

In consequence, g-C3N4 was decorated with CDs frequently to construct heterogeneous structure for optimizing photocatalytic performance.[51] The anchoring of CDs onto g-C3N4 surface contributes to enhancing photocatalytic water splitting activities, as initially evidenced by Liu et al.[52] The integration of CDs to carbon nitride by Wu et al. led to good performance for photocatalytic PPCPs degradation under broad-spectrum light illumination (from UV light to NIR light).[53] Basically, the introduction of CDs increased the light-harvesting ability, as well as the construction of heterogeneous systems facilitated the photoinduced charge separation. However, due to the problem of inherent hydrophobicity, g-C3N4 can hardly construct strong electron coupling with hydrophilic carbon dots, restricting the sufficient charge transfer between two components.

Specifically, heteroatom doping is recognized as an effective strategy to firmly strengthen the hybridization of g-C3N4/CDs composites. Moreover, the electronic structure as well as broaden optical absorption of g-C3N4 can be modulated by doping strategy for enhancing the photocatalytic performance. Scores of studies were carried out on metal-free doping over carbon nitrides. Liu et al. first fabricated sulfur-doped g-C3N4 for enhanced photodegradation activity.[54] Bai et al. constructed Cl doped surface defective graphite carbon nitride with spongy morphology for providing abundant pathways of charge transfer.[55] Metal-free co-doping such as B/F-co-doped g-C3N4 by Ding et al. exhibited more enhanced optical performance and higher photocatalytic activities.[56] Sulfur and chlorine-co-doped g-C3N4 (S/Cl-CN) photocatalysts with improved photocatalytic degradation activity have been fabricated by employing thiourea and ammonium chloride as precursors by Yi et al. [57] Likewise, the heteroatom such as S and O doping makes a difference to electronic structures and optical properties of carbon nitride to be an efficient optional strategy for enhancing catalytic actions in heterogeneous g-C3N4/CDs system.[58,59]

Typically, heteroatoms can help regulate energy-level configuration of g-C3N4, significantly accelerating electron transfer between CDs and doped carbon nitride. More importantly, the photocatalytic performance of heterogeneous composite highly depends on the interfacial interaction between two constituents. The problem that hydrophobic g-C3N4 always tends to establish weak electronic coupling with hydrophilic CDs results in the deficient charge transfer and restricted interfacial attachment between two components. Therefore, the role of heterostructures in the g-C3N4/CDs photocatalytic system fell far short of expectations and the photocatalytic performance can hardly be effectively enhanced. Mou et al. reported that the tight chemical interaction of tris-triazine units and CDs were formed by N-C covalent bonds. In consequence, the strong coupled g-C3N4/CD composites displayed much enhanced photocatalytic activities toward water splitting.[60] Therefore, heteroatom doping is considered as an efficient strategy for enhancing the interface connection to each other in the heterogeneous g-C3N4/CDs system. Hitherto, anchoring CDs on metal-free co-doped graphitic carbon nitride to form chemical covalent bonds for tight coupling of heterogeneous g-C3N4/CDs has never been reported.

Herein, a novel heterogeneous S, Cl co-doped carbon nitride/CDs (CSCl-C3N4) hybrid nanosheets with compact connection via C-Cl chemical bonding was rationally designed and successfully fabricated via a facile thermal polymerization approach. The fabrication approach, conditions and obtained structure of materials in our work are quite distinguishing from the above reports. The CSCl-C3N4 composites possess suitable redox potentials, modulated electronic structure, enhanced light harvest ability, high charge carrier separation efficiency and enlarged specific surface area, simultaneously. A versatile material has been developed in this work for achieving both sunlight-driven photocatalytic degradation of tetracycline and water splitting to generate hydrogen. Accordingly, CSCl-C3N4 exhibits the optimal photocatalytic performance in multi-functional applications in solar energy conversion and pollution abatement in this work. Possible photocatalytic transfer pathway and mechanisms for enhanced photocatalytic TC remediation were investigated via DFT calculations in conjunction with a range of characterizations.
The detailed description of synthetic procedures, general characterizations, photocatalytic performance tests and electrochemical measurements are illustrated in the supporting information.

In the XRD patterns of pristine g-C3N4 and modified carbon nitride, displayed in Fig. 1 a, two typical diffraction peaks are observed at approximately 27.4° and 13.1°, indexed to (002) and (100) planes, ascribed to interplanar stacking of aromatic segments and in-planar repeated tri-s-triazine units.[61] Accordingly, the tri-s-triazine structure of g-C3N4 is retained. No obvious shift of (002) peak can be observed in 0.5CSCl-C3N4, signifying similar interlaminar distances. As illustrated in Fig. 1b, the peaks in 3000–3400 and 808 cm−1 are respectively related to NH stretching vibrations and typical triazine rings, while the sharp bands of pristine carbon nitride in the range of 1230–1626 cm−1 are assigned to stretching vibration modes of aromatic CN and C-N heterocycles.[62] In accordance with the XRD results, the basic chemical structures of carbon nitride are retained due to all of the modified samples possess similar ATR-FTIR spectra to that of pristine carbon nitride.

Specifically, from the AFM images recorded in Fig. 1c, CSCl-C3N4 was successfully exfoliated into two-dimensional nanosheets with a thickness of ∼3.12 nm, consisted of ∼10CN layers.[63] The nanosheets of CSCl-C3N4 composite are successfully obtained due to the fracture of partial hydrogen bonds of interlayers in carbon nitride by secondary calcination.[64] Combined with the results of XRD, CSCl-C3N4 nanosheets possess fewer layers for efficient carrier diffusion. The 0.5CSCl-C3N4 sample exhibits flat surface with distinct edges, indicating good two-dimensional geometry, in accordance with the height profile. Owing to the combination of heteroatom dopants and robust anchoring of CDs, the aggregation of g-C3N4 layers are effectively limited. Accordingly, the diffusion length from bulk to interface has been shortened. More exposed active sites and efficient charge transfer between layers could accelerate photocatalytic reactions.

As observed from TEM images in Fig. 2 a, the laminar CSCl-C3N4 with intact morphology is composed of nanosheets. An amount of mesoporous distributed on the surface of carbon nitride enhance the photoinduced charge transfer. The releasing gases from precursors and oxidation of carbon dots facilitate the formation of pores and modulate the morphology of nanosheets, thereby exposing abundant active sites to photocatalytic reaction. [42,62,65] It is clearly observed that CSCl-C3N4 consists of CD-anchored (red circles) nanosheets from the high resolution TEM (HRTEM) images. The grain-like CDs with average size of 2.2 nm in diameter are distributed on the surface of g-C3N4 matrix, representing a compact interaction between interfaces to facilitate the migration of charge carriers in photocatalytic reactions. In Fig. 2b, the distinct lattice fringes correspond to (101) lattice plane of graphitic carbon with an interplanar spacing of 0.203 nm, demonstrating robust anchoring of CDs onto the surface of carbon nitride and g-C3N4/CDs heterogeneous composite is successfully constructed.[52]

Revealed from SEM images in Fig. S1(supporting information), pristine g-C3N4 displays a distinct lamellar-like morphology with smooth surface, while the other modified carbon nitrides possess an irregular stacked nanosheets with curved surface, in accordance with the typical laminar morphology of g-C3N4. Energy-dispersive spectroscopy (EDS) elemental mapping images from selected areas in CSCl-C3N4 reveal the distribution of C, N, S, Cl elements, demonstrating the successful doping of heteroatoms. The doped S and Cl are evenly dispersed within the carbon nitride matrix.

Subsequently, the specific BET surface area (SBET) and porosity of carbon nitrides were examined by N2 adsorption-desorption isotherms. As illustrated in Fig. 3 a, all samples exhibit type IV isotherms with H3 hysteresis loops, revealing the mesoporous structures.[66] The SBET of pristine C3N4 and 0.5CSCl-C3N4 composite are calculated to be 10.47 and 34.81 m2/g, respectively (Table S1, supporting information). Evidently, the introduction of carbon dots for co-doped sample achieve the significantly enhanced specific surface area among carbon nitrides. Moreover, the enlarged SBET and pore size of doped carbon nitrides could be attributed to the released gases (HCl, H2S and NH3) from the dopants in calcining process.[65,67] Accordingly, sufficient active sites are provided for photocatalytic reactions due to the enlarged specific surface area, corresponding to the analysis of AFM and TEM. The BJH pore size distribution further verifying the mesoporous structure of carbon nitride, in consistent with TEM observation. As expected, 0.5CSCl-C3N4 possesses much larger pore volume (0.35 cm3 g−1) relative to pristine C3N4 (0.10 cm3 g−1).

X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was performed to explore the chemical environment and bonding state of as-synthesized samples. The N/C ratio decrease from 1.323 to 1.148 for 0.5CSCl-C3N4 and pristine carbon nitride, demonstrating the introduction of surface nitrogen defects. The C 1 s spectra were deconvoluted to three peaks at 288.1, 286.2 and 284.6 eV (Fig. 3c), ascribed to sp2-hybridized C in the aromatic rings (N-CN), C-NHx on the edges of aromatic rings and CC covalent bonds, respectively.[68] Compared with pristine carbon nitride, the weakened peak at about 286.2 eV of 0.5CSCl-C3N4 reveals the decreased C-NHx. Consequently, 0.5CSCl-C3N4 composite probably possess an enhanced degree of polymerization as well as the reduced hydrogen bonds intra layers for facilitating charge transfer, consisted with the annealing process and results of AFM. The N 1 s spectrum of pristine carbon nitride is deconvoluted into three peaks (Fig. 3d). The peak at 400.6 eV corresponds to NHx groups, while the other two peaks at 399.3 and 398.4 eV are ascribed to pyrrolic nitrogen (N–(C)3) and pyridinic nitrogen (CNC), respectively.[69] In the case of 0.5CSCl-C3N4 heterogeneous material, all the N 1 s peaks of main species represent evident blue shifts to lower values of binding energies, without emerging new peaks, indicative of the increased electron density of N atoms and variation of chemical environment. The electron cloud density in conjugated systems can be effectively increased by N3C due to lone pair electrons in the p orbital. The content of N3C of 0.5CSCl-C3N4 increases remarkably (from 0.16 to 0.26), further providing evidence for nitrogen defects and increased charge density (Table S2). The intensity of N2C decreases due to the corporation of CDs, evidencing the formation of nitrogen defects likewise. Moreover, the peak at 532.9 eV assigned to CO can be observable in the case of 0.5CSCl-C3N4 composite due to the abundant carboxy in carbon dots (Fig. S5, supporting information).[70]

A peak at 164.3 eV in the S 2p spectrum is ascribed to S-C covalent bond, indicating sulfur atoms were successfully doped into 0.5CSCl-C3N4 composite via substituting for lattice nitrogen.[62,71] Evidently, the Cl 2p spectrum of 0.5CSCl-C3N4 composite was deconvoluted into three peaks at near 200.8, 199.2 and 197.5 eV. The peak centered at 200.8 eV corresponds to C-Cl covalent bond, while the peaks at 199.2 and 197.5 eV are ascribed to chloride ions on the surface.[55,72] No peaks corresponding to C-Cl bond are observed in the Cl 2p spectrum of S, Cl doped g-C3N4 (Fig. S3, supporting information), revealing CDs functioning as a link to interact with chlorine atoms. More importantly, C-Cl covalent bond endows the robust anchoring of CDs on g-C3N4 and compact interfacial attachment of heterojunctions.

Subsequently, typical semiconductor absorption in the blue region of all the samples are detected in ultraviolet-visible diffusion reflectance spectra (UV-vis DRS).[73-75] The light capturing ability in visible region of modified carbon nitride is markedly enhanced, indicating the potential to possess considerable photocatalytic activity. With respect to pristine carbon nitride, the absorption edges of S-C3N4 and SCl-C3N4 exhibit redshifts due to introduction of dopants. Indeed, the enhanced optical properties and modulated electronic structure of carbon nitrides are achieved by heteroatoms co-doping. The absorption edge of 0.5CSCl-C3N4 composites redshifts to 515 nm and displays intense absorption in visible region, assigned to characteristics of CDs for broadening the visible light spectral response. Simultaneously, it is represented the compact interface interaction of C3N4 and CDs. Nevertheless, Cl-C3N4 displays a slight blueshift probably owing to the quantum confinement effect of semiconductor nanomaterials during the synthesis process with guanidine hydrochloride.[76] The incorporation of CDs and doping of S, Cl into C3N4 matrix leads to a significant enhancement in optical absorption over the wide visible region. As expected, the Eg value decreases from 2.75 eV for pristine carbon nitride to 2.66 eV for 0.5CSCl-C3N4 (Fig. S6). The anchoring of CDs optimizes the electronic structure for narrowing bandgap of 0.5CSCl-C3N4, increasing the charge mobility in photocatalytic redox reactions.

DFT calculations of modified carbon nitride were further performed to obtain reasonable structure model and understand the location of doping heteroatoms. It was conducted to investigate each doping sites of S and Cl in the CSCl-C3N4 sample for supposing the structure of materials. According to the DFT calculation results (Fig. 5) based on the perfect crystal of the g-C3N4, there are two kinds of possible occupying positions for heteroatoms, namely substitutional sites and interstitial sites. Fig. 5 lists as many probable structures as possible, and the structure with the lowest energy is represented in Fig. 5f, namely the most stable and optimized structure for photocatalytic reactions. Importantly, these molecules possess different structures three-dimensionally, such as bond lengths, bond angles and dihedral angles. Due to the lowest formation energy for doping situation calculation of CSCl-C3N4 structure in Fig. 5f, the S atoms occupy the substituted sites of N atoms on the corner, whereas the interstitial doping of Cl atoms occurs on the interlayer of g-C3N4, cohering with the XPS results. From XPS results of the observation of S-C bonds in 0.5CSCl-C3N4 sample, S atoms substitute the N sites, in line with the existence of N deficiency. Therefore, the represented structure in Fig. 5f is most likely for CSCl-C3N4 sample.

Besides modulating the optical properties and increasing light harvesting ability of composites, the inhibition capability of photoinduced charge carrier recombination is expected to be facilitated. Steady-state photoluminescence (PL) emissions of 0.5CSCl-C3N4, SCl-C3N4, S-C3N4, Cl-C3N4 and pristine g-C3N4 were carried out to evaluate the charge transfer and recombination efficiency of electron-hole pairs. Distinct from the strong PL emission peak at 460 nm of pristine carbon nitride, significantly quenched PL signals of S-doped C3N4 and Cl-doped C3N4 can be observed in Fig. 4
c, indicative of more trapping sites and defects derived from doped heteroatoms for suppressed recombination of photoinduced charge carriers. Further on, upon anchoring of CDs with varying contents, the PL intensity decreased remarkably with the increased concentration of CDs (Fig. S7). The florescence lifetimes of 0.5CSCl-C3N4 and pristine g-C3N4 are 5.53 ns and 4.30 ns, respectively. It is indicated that CDs/g-C3N4 heterostructure efficiently inhibits the recombination of charge carriers for generation of more active species to participate in photocatalytic reactions. The coupling interaction at the heterogeneous interface between CDs and C3N4, serving as transfer channels of charges for driving charge delocalization around the photoexcited sites.

Furthermore, to evaluate separation behaviors of photogenerated charge carriers, photoelectrochemical experiments were carried out at 0.1 M sodium sulfate solution in a three-electrode system. Fig. 6 a illustrates the transient photocurrent response of 0.5CSCl-C3N4, SCl-C3N4, S-C3N4, Cl-C3N4 and pristine g-C3N4 via continuous on-off light cycles. Notably, the highest photocurrent response at every on-off light cycle was detected for 0.5CSCl-C3N4 under visible light illumination, indicative of effectively prolonged lifetime and enhanced separation efficiency of photoinduced charge carriers. Electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) of as-prepared samples are recorded in Fig. 6b. Basically, with reference to pristine C3N4, the S-doped, Cl-doped as well as S, Cl doped samples display smaller arc radius of Nyquist plots, indicative of the lower resistance for charge transfer and acceleration of redox reactions by S and Cl doping. Distinctly, 0.5CSCl-C3N4 exhibits the smallest arc radius due to the coupling interaction at CDs/C3N4 interface and good conductivity of CDs, in agreement with the results of transient photocurrent.

CDs were successfully anchored onto the S, Cl co-doped carbon nitride via chemical bond interaction to construct the heterostructure on the basis of above results. This strong heterogeneous interaction effectively facilitates the charge carrier separation. Combining with the expanded light harvest, increased electronic channels and evidently changed morphology, the visible light catalytic performance of CSCl-C3N4 composite is expected to be effectively improved.

As expected, the 0.5CSCl-C3N4 composite achieves the optimized H2 evolution rate of 192.49 μmol∙h−1 among SCl-C3N4 (96.5 μmol∙h−1), S-C3N4 (38.49 μmol∙h−1), Cl-C3N4 (46.11 μmol∙h−1) and 0.5C-C3N4 (49.65 μmol∙h−1), about 25 times higher than that of pristine carbon nitride (7.74 μmol∙h−1). The above results corroborate the necessity of combination of heteroatom dopants and CDs in carbon nitrides.

Moreover, experimental results explore that hydrogen evolution rate highly depends on the CDs contents. Basically, the H2 evolution rates of CSCl-C3N4 are enhanced from 96.5 to 192.49 μmol∙h−1 with varying the contents of CDs loaded (0–0.5%). Nevertheless, as the amounts of CDs increase (0.5%∼2.0%), the water splitting toward H2 evolution decreases to 127.53 μmol∙h−1 under visible light irradiation (Fig. S8). It has been reported that the high content of CDs around composites can result in a competition for visible light absorption, which can explain the phenomenon that the visible light photocatalytic activity decreased. [77,78] The differences of photocatalytic performance of composites consisting of pristine g-C3N4 and the carbon dots, the sulfur doped g-C3N4 and the carbon dots (CS-C3N4), and the chlorine doped g-C3N4 and the carbon dots (CCl-C3N4) were analyzed. The H2 evolution rate of 0.5CS-C3N4 compared with that of 0.5CSCl-C3N4 demonstrates the role of Cl dopant, while the photocatalytic performance of 0.5CCl-C3N4 highlights the contribution of S doping. Notably, as observed the results in Fig. S14, the doping of Cl may have a greater effect on the performance. The H2 evolution rates of single-doped samples (S-C3N4 and Cl-C3N4) were moderate, yet the SCl-C3N4 exhibited considerable activities, indicative of the role of co-doping. Moreover, the H2 evolution rate increased from 38.49 μmol h−1 (S-C3N4) to 54.07 μmol h−1 (0.5CS-C3N4), whereas the performance enhanced from 46.11 μmol h−1 (Cl-C3N4) to 93.48 μmol h−1 (0.5CCl-C3N4). It is demonstrated that the Cl doping does affect the CDs. Selective photo-deposition of noble metal is employed to investigate the surface properties of a given semiconductor. The noble metal tends to deposit at the electron-rich region of the semiconductor during the photodeposition process. Herein, the photodeposition of Pt nanoparticles on the 0.5CSCl-C3N4 composites was investigated by TEM. Two lattice fringes can be observed in Fig. S18, the partial Pt nanoparticles are loaded on the CDs surface, indicating that the electrons can be accumulated on the CDs surface. Accordingly, Pt generated in situ will also be deposited on CDs surface to accomplish electron transfer in the photocatalytic reaction process.

Further on, the obtained materials represent considerable photocatalytic performance in contaminant decomposition besides solar fuels production. In the case of pristine carbon nitride, only 40% of TC was photodegraded in 40 min. Significantly, 84% of TC has been decomposed in the case of SCl-C3N4 within the same irradiation duration, simultaneously more than that with single-doped samples S-C3N4 and Cl-C3N4. Notably, the photo-degradation of TC was remarkably facilitated upon anchoring of CDs. For instance, the composites anchored with a low content of CDs (0.5 wt%) achieved 93% removal of TC, exhibiting the optimal performance of photocatalytic removal among S-C3N4, Cl-C3N4, SCl-C3N4 and 0.5C-C3N4 samples. Total organic carbon (TOC) is generally used to measure the organic content in the photocatalytic process, thus further evaluate the degree of mineralization in organic degradation. The TOCs of TC solution during the photocatalysis were further tested (Fig. S17). In the case of 0.5CSCl-C3N4, the TOC of TC in the final degraded solution is as low as 38.5% at the light irradiation time of 40 min, demonstrating 0.5CSCl-C3N4 possesses a considerable ability of mineralization under visible light irradiation.

The kinetics of photocatalytic TC degradation is investigated by using pseudo-first and pseudo-second order kinetic models, respectively. The correlation coefficients (R2) in Table S3 (supporting information) demonstrate that the pseudo-second order kinetic model is fitted better to describe photocatalytic process of decomposing TC than pseudo-first order kinetic model. As displayed in Fig. 7 b, the value of kinetic rate constant k of 0.5CSCl-C3N4 (0.28007 L·mg−1·min−1) for TC degradation is 15.3 times higher than that of pristine C3N4 (0.0183 L·mg−1·min−1), achieving the remarkably highest photodegradation rate among carbon nitride samples. Likewise, among the other CSCl-C3N4 composites with varying contents of CDs, 0.5CSCl-C3N4 still exhibits optimal photocatalytic degradation rate for the removal of TC (Fig. S9). The kinetic rate constant of CSCl-C3N4 composites decrease from 0.280 to 0.105 L·mg−1·min−1 with the addition of CDs from 0.5% to 2.0%, while the k value for TC degradation over 0.1CSCl-C3N4 is only 0.075 L·mg−1·min−1. The trend in Fig. S10 further illustrates that a suitable content of CDs is required for enhancing photocatalytic performance, in accordance with photocatalytic H2 evolution performance. Therefore, 0.5CSCl-C3N4 composite was selected for all relevant characterizations. Furthermore, the stability of the optimized 0.5CSCl-C3N4 was verified by cycling runs experiments. The photocatalytic activity of 0.5CSCl-C3N4 was fairly consistent even after four cycles, which illustrated that the nanocomposite was recyclable and actively stable.

The band structures of 0.5CSCl-C3N4 and pristine carbon nitride were confirmed to explore the photocatalytic mechanism of 0.5CSCl-C3N4 composites for H2 evolution and TC degradation. The calculated band gaps for pristine g-C3N4 and 0.5CSCl-C3N4 are 2.75 and 2.66 eV, respectively. The positive slopes of curves with various frequencies of Mott-Schottky plots demonstrate typical n-type semiconductor characteristics. Notably, the value of slope over 0.5CSCl-C3N4 composite becomes smaller, representing faster migration and separation of charge carriers.[63] The flat band potential is −0.89 V for pristine g-C3N4 and −0.97 V for 0.5CSCl-C3N4 (vs. Ag/AgCl), respectively. The conduction band potentials calculated from Nernst equation are −0.69 V and −0.77 V vs. NHE. The potential exhibits negative shift due to the incorporation of heteroatoms and CDs, signifying that electrons in conduction band of 0.5CSCl-C3N4 are more active for higher reduction ability to split water easily. According to the formula: EVB = ECB + Eg, the calculated valence band potentials are 2.06 V for pristine g-C3N4 and 1.89 V for 0.5CSCl-C3N4, respectively.

Triethanolamine (TEOA), isopropanol (IPA) and p-benzoquinone (p-BQ) served as scavengers to quench holes (h+), hydroxyl radical (∙OH) and superoxide radical (∙O2 −) during photocatalytic degradation of TC, respectively. Observed from Fig. 8 b, following the addition of TEOA and IPA, the photocatalytic efficiency decreased moderately, whereas p-BQ exhibits a remarkable inhibiting effect on TC decomposition, signifying that ∙O2 − predominates toward photocatalytic TC degradation.

Electron paramagnetic resonance (EPR) was measured to further determine the existence of ∙O2 − and ∙OH in the photodegradation process. As illustrated in Fig. 8c and d, no characteristic peaks of reactive oxygen species were detected in dark. In contrast, distinct signals of ∙OH and ∙O2 − radicals over 0.5CSCl-C3N4 are observed under visible light illumination, indicative of the existence of ∙O2 − and ∙OH in 0.5CSCl-C3N4 heterogeneous photocatalytic system.

A probable schematic mechanism of band structure alignments was proposed on the basis of above analyses, illustrated in Scheme 1 . S and Cl co-doping in carbon nitride modulates the electronic structure and shift conduction band to a more negative potential, demonstrating the enhanced optical properties and higher reduction ability. Meanwhile, due to the desirable up-converted photoluminescence (UCPL) of CDs (“artificial antennas”), long-wavelength visible and NIR light could be efficiently transformed to short light wavelengths.[79] The formation of photogenerated electron-hole pairs is significantly facilitated owing to the CDs and porous nanosheet structures.

The high reduction ability of 0.5CSCl-C3N4 contributes to activating the electrons in conduction band. Due to the nanosheet structures and high work functions of carbon dots, the photoexcited electrons could be effectively stored within CDs for photoinduced charge separation.[53] O2 could be spontaneously trapped by photogenerated electrons to give rise to ∙O2 − on account of the reduction potential of O2/∙O2 − (-0.33 eV).

The holes at valence band are unable to oxidize OH− to ∙OH directly based on the EVB of 0.5CSCl-C3N4 (1.89 eV) is more negative than the standard redox potential of OH−/∙OH (2.27 eV). Yet the generation of ∙OH in the 0.5CSCl-C3N4 system could proceed by following equations:[53,80]
(1)
·
O 2 -

H 2

H -

(2)

Consequently, the extensive surface area of 0.5CSCl-C3N4 nanosheets contributing to absorbing more TC molecules. Thereby the reactive species ∙O2 −, h+, ∙OH could attack adsorbed PPCPs contaminants.

In summary, a novel metal-free heterogeneous CSCl-C3N4 composite with tight chemical connection has been successfully fabricated via a facile calcination strategy without other templates. The obtained 0.5CSCl-C3N4 achieves the strong chemical interaction between hydrophobic carbon nitride and carbon dots with hydrophilia. Therefore, 0.5CSCl-C3N4 heterogeneous system represents optimal photocatalytic performance both on tetracycline degradation (0.28007 L·mg−1·min−1) and H2 evolution (192.49 μmol∙h−1) under visible light illumination, enhanced 25 and 15.3 times, respectively. The porous CSCl-C3N4 nanosheets possess efficient photoexcited carrier separation, modified electron structure, suitable redox potential, accelerated charge carrier transfer and enhanced light harvest ability. These advantages mentioned are attributed to anchoring of CDs to form composites with covalent chemical bonds and S, Cl co-doping. This study provides a promising strategy for the exploration of responsive broad-spectrum heterogeneous systems with nanostructural and tight-coupled advantages, while demonstrating promising potentials for water purification and solar conversion applications.
